title: Alt-Tab

content.problemStatement:
Gigi got bored and started to open/reopen some programs on his computer.

By reopening a program, we mean that the program was previously opened, wasn't closed in the meantime, and is brought into attention again.

After a while, Gigi wants to know the order in which the programs show up if he were to press `alt + tab`.

Note that the `alt + tab` function displays a list of all **unique** active programs where the **most recently opened** or **reopened** program occupies the top of the list.

Given that Gigi opened/reopened NNN programs where the name of the ithi^{th}ith program is denoted by SiS_iSi​, find the order in which the programs are displayed when pressing `alt + tab`.
constraints: 1 <= N <= 40000 (sum of N over all test cases <= 40000)
Si contains only uppercase English letters
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of program openings/reopenings. Then N lines follow, each containing a program name Si consisting of only uppercase English letters (2 ≤ |Si| ≤ 45).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the concatenation of the last two characters of each distinct active program, ordered from the most recently opened/reopened to the least recent.

solution.approach: Traverse the list of program names from the end to the beginning while maintaining a hash set of already seen program names. When a name is encountered for the first time (i.e., not in the set), add its last two characters to the answer. This directly yields the order required by the alt+tab list because the most recent occurrence of each distinct program determines its position. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case with O(N) additional memory for the set and the stored names.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the input names and the hash set of seen programs.

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i: test case index (0 <= i <= 20)
    # RINT(a,b): random integer in [a,b]
    # POWER(a,b): a**b
    if i == 0:
        print(1)
        print(1)
        print('AB')
    elif i == 1:
        print(1)
        print(1)
        print('XY')
    # Add further generation logic for other indices as needed
    # For simplicity, this stub only handles the first two cases.
